10 Lecture 9: Multi-Step Methods and Runge-Kutta Methods 10.1 1. Multi-Step Methods 10.2 2. Review: Key Concepts for Solving IVPs 10.3 3. Runge-Kutta Methods 10.4 4. Adaptive Time Stepping 10.5 5. Operator Splitting

10 Lecture 9: Multi-Step Methods and Runge-Kutta Methods 10.1 1. Multi-Step Methods Multi-step methods achieve higher order of accuracy by using information from multiple previous time steps. 10.1.1 Definition An \(m\)-step method (\(m \geq 1\)) is a method for which, for all \(n \geq m - 1\), \(u_{n+1}\) depends on \(u_{n+1-m}\) but not on the values \(u_k\) with \(k < n + 1 - m\). Example (Midpoint method): \(u_{n+1} = u_{n-1} + 2hf_n\), \(n \geq 1\). This is a two-step method. 10.1.2 General Linear Multi-Step Method A linear multi-step method for \(y' = f(t, y)\) has the form: \[\frac{1}{h} \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j u_{n-j} = \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j f_{n-j}\] 10.1.3 1.1 Adams-Bashforth (AB) Methods (Explicit) The multi-step method can be derived from the integration form: \[y_n = y_{n-1} + \int_{t_{n-1}}^{t_n} f(s, y(s))\, ds\] The AB method approximates the integral with a Newton-Cotes formula, using the \(m\) previous steps at \(t_{n-1}, t_{n-2}, \ldots, t_{n-m}\). Recall that interpolation with \(m\) points has an error of \(O(h^m)\), and the integral formula has error \(O(h^{m+1})\). Therefore: \[\frac{y_n - y_{n-1}}{h} = \frac{1}{h} \int_{t_{n-1}}^{t_n} f(s, y(s))\, ds = \sum_{j=1}^{m} b_j f_{n-j} + O(h^m)\] The Adams-Bashforth formula is: \[u_n = u_{n-1} + h \sum_{j=1}^{m} b_j f_{n-j}\] Example: AB-2 (\(m = 2\)) \[u_n = u_{n-1} + h(a f_{n-1} + b f_{n-2})\] We expect \(O(h^2)\) error. Using Taylor series to determine \(a\) and \(b\): LHS: \(\frac{1}{h}(y_n - y_{n-1}) = y_n' - \frac{h}{2}y_n'' + O(h^2)\) RHS: \(a(y_n' - hy_n'' + O(h^2)) + b(y_n' - 2hy_n'' + O(h^2)) + O(h^2)\) \(= (a + b)y_n' + h(-a - 2b)y_n'' + O(h^2)\) Matching coefficients: \[\begin{cases} a + b = 1 \\ -a - 2b = -\frac{1}{2} \end{cases} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{cases} a = \frac{3}{2} \\ b = -\frac{1}{2} \end{cases}\] \[\boxed{u_n = u_{n-1} + h\left(\frac{3}{2}f_{n-1} - \frac{1}{2}f_{n-2}\right), \quad \tau_n = O(h^2)}\] Remark: The AB approach is popular as it is efficient to compute: to go from \(u_n\) to \(u_{n+1}\), only one function evaluation is required. Yet it requires more than one starting value. The first \(m - 1\) steps must be computed by some other method. 10.1.4 1.2 Adams-Moulton (AM) Methods (Implicit) When approximating \(\int_{t_{n-1}}^{t_n} f(s, y(s))\, ds\), if \(t_n\) is used as one of the interpolation points, the resulting formula is implicit, and is called the Adams-Moulton method. When \(m = 1\), the AM method is the trapezoidal rule: \[u_n = u_{n-1} + \frac{h}{2}(f_{n-1} + f_n)\] In general: \[\frac{1}{h}(y_n - y_{n-1}) = \frac{1}{h}\int_{t_{n-1}}^{t_n} f(s, y(s))\, ds = \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j f_{n-j} + O(h^{m+1})\] The order of this method is \(m + 1\) (one order higher than AB with the same number of steps), because we have an extra interpolation point. 10.1.5 1.3 Backward Differentiation Formula (BDF) Instead of approximating the integral of \(f\), one can also approximate \(y'\) directly. For \(y' = f(t, y)\), use points \(t_n, t_{n-1}, \ldots, t_{n-m}\): \[\frac{1}{h} \sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j y_{n-j} = f_n + \tau_n, \quad \tau_n = O(h^m)\] When \(m = 1\): backward Euler: \(\frac{1}{h}(u_n - u_{n-1}) = f_n\) When \(m = 2\): BDF-2 method: \[\boxed{\frac{1}{2h}(3u_n - 4u_{n-1} + u_{n-2}) = f_n}\] (See textbook p. 502 for details on higher-order BDF.) 10.1.6 1.4 Stability of Multi-Step Methods Main result: Dahlquist Equivalence Theorem A linear multi-step method \(\frac{1}{h}\sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j u_{n-j} = \sum_{j=0}^{m} b_j f_{n-j}\) is zero-stable if and only if all solutions \(u_n\) stay bounded when the method is applied to the trivial ODE \(y' = 0\). That is, it is zero-stable if and only if all solutions to \(\sum_{j=0}^{m} a_j u_{n-j} = 0\) stay bounded as \(n \to \infty\). 10.1.7 Example: BDF-2 Consider the backward differentiation formula: \(\frac{3u_n - 4u_{n-1} + u_{n-2}}{2h} = f(t_n, u_n)\). To check zero-stability, apply it to \(y' = 0\): \[3u_n - 4u_{n-1} + u_{n-2} = 0\] This is a linear constant-coefficient difference equation. The characteristic polynomial is: \[3r^2 - 4r + 1 = 0\] \[r_1 = 1, \quad r_2 = \frac{1}{3}\] General solution: \(u_n = a \cdot 1^n + b \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n\) Since both roots have \(|r| \leq 1\) (and the root of modulus 1 is simple), the solution is bounded \(\Rightarrow\) BDF-2 is zero-stable. 10.2 2. Review: Key Concepts for Solving IVPs To solve the IVP: \(\frac{dy}{dt} = f(t, y), \quad y(0) = y_0\) Method 1: Forward Euler’s method (explicit) Scheme: \(\frac{u_{n+1} - u_n}{h} = f(t_n, u_n)\) Convergence requires: (1) Consistency: \(\text{LTE} = O(h^p)\), and (2) Zero-stability Method 2: Absolute stability When approximately solving \(y' = \lambda y\): \(|u_n| \to 0\) as \(t_n \to \infty\) This depends on both \(\lambda\) and \(h\) For stiff problems, the stability constraint is a significant issue Method 3: Multi-step methods — when we need higher order Use \(u_{n-1}, u_{n-2}, \ldots\) to update to \(u_{n+1}\) Example: AB-2: \(u_n = u_{n-1} + h\left(\frac{3}{2}f_{n-1} - \frac{1}{2}f_{n-2}\right)\) The first \(m - 1\) steps need to be computed with another method 10.3 3. Runge-Kutta Methods 10.3.1 3.1 Motivation and Derivation From the Taylor expansion: \[y_{n+1} = y_n + hy_n' + \frac{h^2}{2}y_n'' + \cdots + \frac{h^p}{p!}y_n^{(p)} + O(h^{p+1}) \tag{1}\] The forward Euler method uses only the first-order term: \[u_{n+1} = u_n + hf(t_n, u_n)\] To derive a method of higher order, we could use multi-step methods. Alternatively, we can use one-step methods with multiple stages. 10.3.2 Computing Higher Derivatives \[y'' = \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} = \frac{d}{dt}(f(t, y(t))) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} = f_t + f_y \cdot f\] where \(f_t = \frac{\partial f}{\partial t}\), \(f_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\). This is undesirable as the expression involves partial derivatives of \(f\), which are complicated to compute. 10.3.3 The Runge-Kutta Approach Two-stage RK: Instead of computing derivatives of \(f\), evaluate \(f\) at multiple points: \[f_1 = f(t_n, y_n)\] \[f_2 = f(t_n + ch, y_n + hbf_1)\] \[y_{n+1} = y_n + h(w_1 f_1 + w_2 f_2) + O(h^3)\] Here \(b, c, w_1, w_2\) all need to be determined to ensure the method is second-order consistent. 10.3.4 Deriving the Conditions We want \(\frac{y_{n+1} - y_n}{h} = w_1 f_1 + w_2 f_2 + \tau_{n+1}\) with \(\tau_{n+1} = O(h^2)\). LHS (Taylor expansion around \((t_n, y_n)\)): \[\frac{1}{h}(y_{n+1} - y_n) = y_n' + \frac{h}{2}y_n'' + O(h^2) = f + \frac{h}{2}(f_t + f \cdot f_y) + O(h^2)\] RHS: \(f_1 = f\) and using 2D Taylor series: \[f_2 = f(t_n + ch, y_n + hbf_1) = f + ch f_t + bhf_1 f_y + O(h^2)\] Thus: \[\text{RHS} = w_1 f + w_2(f + chf_t + bhf \cdot f_y + O(h^2))\] Matching LHS = RHS: \[f + \frac{h}{2}(f_t + f \cdot f_y) + O(h^2) = (w_1 + w_2)f + w_2 ch f_t + w_2 bh f \cdot f_y + O(h^2)\] This gives three constraints with four unknowns: \[\begin{cases} w_1 + w_2 = 1 \\ c w_2 = \frac{1}{2} \\ b w_2 = \frac{1}{2} \end{cases}\] Thus we have one free parameter. Let \(w_2 = \theta\), \(w_1 = 1 - \theta\), \(c = b = \frac{1}{2\theta}\). For any \(\theta \neq 0\): \[f_1 = f(t_n, y_n)\] \[f_2 = f\!\left(t_n + \frac{h}{2\theta}, \; y_n + \frac{h}{2\theta}f_1\right)\] \[y_{n+1} = y_n + h\big(\theta f_1 + (1 - \theta)f_2\big) + O(h^3)\] Question: Would any choice of \(\theta\) lead to higher order? In practice, no. 10.3.5 The Improved Euler Method A special case with \(\theta = 1/2\) (\(w_1 = w_2 = 1/2\), \(c = b = 1\)): \[k_1 = f(x_n, y_n)\] \[u_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot k_1\] \[k_2 = f(x_{n+1}, u_{n+1})\] \[y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot \frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2)\] This is also known as Heun’s method. 10.3.6 3.2 The Classical RK-4 Method We can use more stages, giving more free parameters and leading to several “families” of methods. The classical RK-4 method: \[f_1 = f(t_n, y_n)\] \[f_2 = f\!\left(t_n + \frac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \frac{h}{2}f_1\right)\] \[f_3 = f\!\left(t_n + \frac{h}{2}, \; y_n + \frac{h}{2}f_2\right)\] \[f_4 = f(t_n + h, \; y_n + hf_3)\] \[\boxed{y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}(f_1 + 2f_2 + 2f_3 + f_4) + O(h^5)}\] Remark: This is an order 4 method. It provides an optimal gain in efficiency per step: each step requires 4 function evaluations and achieves fourth-order accuracy. 10.3.7 3.3 General Explicit RK Methods and Butcher Tableau Definition (RK Methods): A general explicit Runge-Kutta (RK) method uses \(m\) “stages” to get from \(u_n\) to \(u_{n+1}\) and has the form: \[f_1 = f(t_n, u_n)\] \[f_2 = f(t_n + c_2 h, \; u_n + ha_{21}f_1)\] \[f_3 = f(t_n + c_3 h, \; u_n + ha_{31}f_1 + ha_{32}f_2)\] \[\vdots\] \[f_m = f(t_n + c_m h, \; u_n + ha_{m1}f_1 + \cdots + ha_{m,m-1}f_{m-1})\] \[u_{n+1} = u_n + h(w_1 f_1 + \cdots + w_m f_m)\] Each stage \(f_j\) is an evaluation of \(f\) at an intermediate point \((t_n + c_j h, \tilde{u}_j)\) where \(\tilde{u}_j\) involves a linear combination of previous \(f\)’s. The next value \(u_{n+1}\) is a weighted average of the \(f\)’s. 10.3.8 Butcher Tableau The RK method is compactly represented by a Butcher tableau: \[\begin{array}{c|cccc} c_1 & & & & \\ c_2 & a_{21} & & & \\ c_3 & a_{31} & a_{32} & & \\ \vdots & \vdots & & \ddots & \\ c_m & a_{m1} & a_{m2} & \cdots & a_{m,m-1} \\ \hline & w_1 & w_2 & \cdots & w_{m-1} & w_m \end{array}\] For explicit RK methods, the coefficient matrix is strictly lower triangular. For implicit RK methods, it is a full matrix. (See textbook p. 525 for the Butcher tableau of implicit RK methods.) 10.3.9 Theorem (Butcher) An \(m\)-stage RK method can be at best \(m\)-th order: For \(m \leq 4\), the best possible order is exactly \(m\). For \(m > 4\), the best possible order is strictly less than \(m\). For instance, \(m = 6\) stages are required to get a fifth-order method. Thus the sweet spot for RK methods is around 4 stages (RK-4). 10.3.10 3.4 Convergence and Stability of Explicit RK Methods In an explicit Runge-Kutta method: \[u_{n+1} = u_n + h\,\Psi(h, t_n, u_n)\] where \(\Psi\) encapsulates all the intermediate quantities derived from \(f\). Consistency: Automatic from the derivation. Zero-stability: If \(\Psi\) is Lipschitz in \(y\), the scheme is zero-stable (as proved for Euler’s method). If \(f(t, y)\) is Lipschitz in \(y\), so is \(\Psi\). 10.3.11 Absolute Stability of RK Methods Consider the test equation \(y' = \lambda y\), let \(z = h\lambda\). For an \(m\)-stage explicit RK method, each stage \(hf_j\) is a polynomial of degree \(j\) in \(z\) times \(u_n\). Thus: \[u_{n+1} = u_n + \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j(hf_j) = g(z)\, u_n\] where \(g(z)\) is a polynomial of degree \(m\) in \(z\). The stability region is: \[\mathcal{R} = \{z \in \mathbb{C} : |g(z)| \leq 1\}\] Since \(|g(z)| \sim C|z|^m\) as \(|z| \to \infty\), and if \(|g(z)| > 1\), then \(u_n\) grows unbounded. Thus the region of absolute stability is bounded for all explicit RK methods. All explicit RK methods have a stability constraint on \(h\). 10.4 4. Adaptive Time Stepping 10.4.1 Motivation Question: How do we choose grid size \(h\)? Smaller \(h \to\) better accuracy + longer run time Need to ensure stability Since for a one-step method, only the values at the previous step (step \(n\)) are used to update to \(n + 1\), we can choose a new \(h\) for each step. 10.4.2 Algorithm Given an error tolerance \(\varepsilon\): Take a single step of size \(h_0\) Estimate the error between the exact solution and the updated value If error is larger than \(\varepsilon\) \(\Rightarrow\) retry the step with a smaller step size (e.g., \(h_0/2\)) If error is smaller than \(\varepsilon\), accept it and go back to (1) 10.4.3 Error Estimation via Two Methods To estimate the error, we use two numerical methods of different order to approximate the exact solution: Method A (order \(p\)): produces \(u_n\) from \(u_{n-1}\), with local error \(e_n(h) = Ch^{p+1} + O(h^{p+2})\) Method B (order \(p + 1\)): produces \(\tilde{u}_n\) from \(u_{n-1}\), with local error \(\tilde{e}_n(h) = O(h^{p+2})\) Assuming the value at time \(n - 1\) is exact: \[y(t_n) = u_n + e_n(h) = u_n + Ch^{p+1} + O(h^{p+2})\] \[y(t_n) = \tilde{u}_n + \tilde{e}_n(h) = \tilde{u}_n + O(h^{p+2})\] Therefore: \[Ch^{p+1} + O(h^{p+2}) = e_n(h) = \tilde{u}_n - u_n + O(h^{p+2})\] So \(\tilde{u}_n - u_n\) approximates the leading-order term of \(e_n(h)\). Check if \(|\tilde{u}_n - u_n|\) is bounded by \(\varepsilon\) to decide whether to accept or reject the step. 10.4.4 Embedded Pairs In general, the “two method” approach requires twice as much work as using one fixed step. We can create overlap in the computation to save work. Example: Forward Euler (Method A, order 1) and Modified Euler (Method B, order 2): Method A: \(u_{n+1} = u_n + hf(t_n, u_n)\) Method B: \(f_1 = f(t_n, u_n)\) \(f_2 = f(t_n + h/2, \; u_n + \frac{h}{2}f_1)\) \(\tilde{u}_{n+1} = u_n + hf_2\) Only two function evaluations total are done because \(f_1\) is shared between both methods (Euler method is embedded in the modified one). 10.4.5 Practical Pairs Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg and Dormand-Prince pairs use a pair of fourth/fifth order RK methods to adaptively select step size. These are the default choice for solving ODEs in most software (e.g., MATLAB’s ode45). 10.5 5. Operator Splitting An idea widely used in numerical analysis for solving complex problems. 10.5.1 Motivation Consider a PDE as a simple example: \[\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \mathcal{L}_1 u + \mathcal{L}_2 u\] For instance, a convection-diffusion equation: \[\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -c\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + k\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\] One can solve two simpler equations separately: \[\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -c\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = k\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\] Taking one step for the convection part, then one step for the diffusion part. 10.5.2 Analysis: Splitting Error There will be error associated with splitting. Consider a simplified problem: \[\frac{d\vec{y}}{dt} = A\vec{y} + B\vec{y}, \quad y(0) = y_0\] Even simpler: \(\frac{dy}{dt} = ay + by\), \(y(0) = y_0\). If we solve \(y' = by\), get \(w(t) = e^{bt}y_0\), then solve \(y' = ay\): \[y(t) = e^{at}e^{bt}y_0\] For the matrix form: if \(\vec{w}\) solves \(\vec{y}' = B\vec{y}\), then \(\vec{w} = e^{Bt}\vec{y}_0\). Let \(\vec{y}\) solve \(\vec{y}' = A\vec{y}\) with initial condition \(\vec{w}\): \[\vec{y} = e^{At}e^{Bt}\vec{y}_0\] The exact solution should be \(\vec{y} = e^{(A+B)t}\vec{y}_0\). Key issue: \(e^{(A+B)t} \neq e^{At} \cdot e^{Bt}\) in general (unless \(A\) and \(B\) commute). 10.5.3 Order of Splitting Using the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff theorem: \(e^X e^Y = e^Z\) where: \[Z = X + Y + \frac{1}{2}[X, Y] + \frac{1}{12}[X, [X, Y]] + \frac{1}{12}[Y, [Y, X]] + \cdots\] with \([X, Y] = XY - YX\) (the Lie bracket/commutator). Therefore: \(e^{hA}e^{hB} = e^{h(A+B) + O(h^2)}\) We can expect local error of \(O(h^2)\), so the method is order 1. 10.5.4 Combining with Numerical Methods When \(A\) and \(B\) are not constant-coefficient matrices, we can replace \(e^{hA}\) and \(e^{hB}\) by numerical schemes. If we use forward Euler: \[\vec{w}_n = \vec{u}_{n-1} + hB\vec{u}_{n-1} = (I + hB)\vec{u}_{n-1}\] \[\vec{u}_n = \vec{w}_n + hA\vec{w}_n = (I + hA)(I + hB)\vec{u}_{n-1}\] Compare splitting version vs. forward Euler without splitting: \[\vec{u}_n = [I + h(A + B)]\vec{u}_{n-1} \quad \text{(forward Euler)}\] \[(I + hA)(I + hB) = I + h(A + B) + O(h^2) \quad \text{(splitting)}\] The splitting version has \(O(h^2)\) additional local error, matching the order of the splitting itself. 10.5.5 Strang Splitting (Second-Order) Instead of \(e^{hA}e^{hB}\), use: \[e^{\frac{h}{2}A}\, e^{hB}\, e^{\frac{h}{2}A}\] This achieves \(O(h^3)\) local error (i.e., second-order method), providing a significant improvement in accuracy.

11 Lecture 10 (first part review) 11.1 Forward Euler with Splitting 11.2 Strong (Strang) Splitting 11.3 Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff (BCH) Theorem 11.4 Motivating Example: Stiff Equations

11 Lecture 10 (first part review) 11.1 Forward Euler with Splitting Consider an ODE of the form: \[\dot{u} = (A + B)u\] where \(A\) and \(B\) may be matrices (or operators). When \(A\) and \(B\) are not constant, we replace \(e^{hA}\) and \(e^{hB}\) by numerical approximations. Forward Euler splitting gives: \[u_{n+1} = (I + hA)(I + hB)\, u_n\] This scheme has local truncation error \(\mathcal{O}(h^2)\) and global error \(\mathcal{O}(h)\), so the method is first-order accurate. 11.2 Strong (Strang) Splitting To improve accuracy to second order, we use Strang splitting: Take a half step of size \(h/2\) with operator \(A\) Take a full step of size \(h\) with operator \(B\) Take another half step of size \(h/2\) with operator \(A\) \[u_{n+1} = e^{hA/2}\, e^{hB}\, e^{hA/2}\, u_n\] Key requirement: Each matrix exponential approximation must itself be second-order accurate for the overall method to be second order. 11.3 Baker–Campbell–Hausdorff (BCH) Theorem The BCH theorem (from Lie algebra) quantifies the error in operator splitting. If \(X, Y\) are elements in a Lie algebra with commutator \([X,Y] = XY - YX\), then: \[e^X e^Y = e^{Z}, \quad Z = X + Y + \tfrac{1}{2}[X,Y] + \tfrac{1}{12}[X,[X,Y]] - \tfrac{1}{12}[Y,[X,Y]] + \cdots\] The splitting error is \(\mathcal{O}(h^2)\) for standard splitting and \(\mathcal{O}(h^3)\) for Strang splitting. 11.4 Motivating Example: Stiff Equations Consider a stiff ODE: \[u' = -100u + \sin(u)\] Using an explicit method requires a very small step size \(h\) to remain within the stability region — computationally expensive. Solution: Use operator splitting to handle the stiff part implicitly: \[y' = \underbrace{-100y}_{Ay \text{ (stiff, implicit)}} + \underbrace{\sin(y)}_{By \text{ (non-stiff, explicit)}}\] Splitting scheme: \[w_{n+1/2} = y_n + h \cdot By_n \quad \text{(explicit step for } \sin(y)\text{)}\] \[y_{n+1} - 100h\, y_{n+1} = w_{n+1/2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 + 100h)\,y_{n+1} = w_{n+1/2} \quad \text{(implicit step)}\] Advantage: The implicit solve for the stiff part is just a linear equation — no Newton iteration needed. In general, for \(y' = Ay + g(t,y)\), if \(A\) is the stiff part, solve it implicitly to avoid Newton iterations.
introduction-to-boundary-value-problems-bvp.html
12 Introduction to Boundary Value Problems (BVP) 12.1 Definition 12.2 Converting to First-Order Systems 12.3 Why BVPs Are Harder Than IVPs

12 Introduction to Boundary Value Problems (BVP) 12.1 Definition A Boundary Value Problem (BVP) consists of: An ODE defined on some interval \([a, b]\) Boundary conditions involving data at both endpoints Example (second-order BVP): \[y'' = f(x, y, y'), \quad y(a) = c, \quad y(b) = d\] This differs fundamentally from an Initial Value Problem (IVP), where all conditions are given at a single point. 12.2 Converting to First-Order Systems Any higher-order BVP can be rewritten as a system of first-order equations: \[Y' = F(x, Y), \quad x \in [a, b]\] with boundary conditions: \[B\big[Y(a),\, Y(b)\big] = 0\] Example: For \(y'' = f(x, y, y')\) with \(y(a) = c\), \(y(b) = d\), let \(w_1 = y\), \(w_2 = y'\): \[w_1' = w_2, \quad w_2' = f(x, w_1, w_2)\] Boundary conditions become: \[w_1(a) = c, \quad w_1(b) = d\] 12.3 Why BVPs Are Harder Than IVPs For IVPs, we know all initial conditions at one point and step forward to the other end. For BVPs, we have conditions at two different endpoints and cannot simply march forward — this is the fundamental difficulty.
properties-of-bvps.html
13 Properties of BVPs 13.1 Existence and Uniqueness 13.2 Stability

13 Properties of BVPs 13.1 Existence and Uniqueness Unlike IVPs, there is no general existence/uniqueness theory for BVPs. A BVP may have: A unique solution Multiple solutions No solution at all Each equation must be analyzed individually. 13.2 Stability For the linear BVP \(y' = Ay\) with boundary conditions \(B[y(a), y(b)] = 0\): Perturbation analysis: If we add perturbation \(\epsilon(t)\) to the right-hand side: \[y' = Ay + \epsilon(t), \quad B[y(a), y(b)] = \delta\] The solution involves the Green’s function \(G(x, s)\) of the problem: \[y(x) = \int_a^b G(x,s)\, \epsilon(s)\, ds\] Important: Stability for BVPs and IVPs are very different concepts. A BVP can be stable even if the corresponding IVP has exponentially growing solutions, because the boundary condition at \(x = b\) constrains the solution.
method-1-shooting-method.html
14 Method 1: Shooting Method 14.1 Idea 14.2 Algorithm 14.3 Full Workflow Summary 14.4 Required Tools 14.5 Example

14 Method 1: Shooting Method 14.1 Idea The shooting method converts a BVP into an IVP by treating the unknown initial derivative as a parameter \(s\), then finding \(s\) such that the solution matches the boundary condition at the other endpoint. Setup: For the two-point BVP: \[y'' = f(x, y, y'), \quad y(a) = c, \quad y(b) = d\] 14.2 Algorithm 14.2.1 Step 1: Set Up the IVP Define the IVP parameterized by \(s\): \[y'' = f(x, y, y'), \quad y(a) = c, \quad y'(a) = s\] Denote the solution as \(y(x;\, s)\). For some \(s^*\), the solution satisfies \(y(b;\, s^*) = d\). 14.2.2 Step 2: Define the Goal Function Define: \[g(s) = y(b;\, s) - d\] We seek \(s^*\) such that \(g(s^*) = 0\). 14.2.3 Step 3: Apply Newton’s Method Newton’s method for solving \(g(s^*) = 0\): \[s_{n+1} = s_n - \frac{g(s_n)}{g'(s_n)}\] Convergence: If \(g(s^*) = 0\) and \(g'(s^*) \neq 0\), Newton’s method converges quadratically in a neighborhood of \(s^*\): \[e_{n+1} \approx C \cdot e_n^2, \quad \text{where } e_n = s_n - s^*\] 14.2.4 Step 4: Compute \(g'(s)\) To apply Newton’s method, we need \(g'(s) = \frac{\partial y}{\partial s}(b;\, s)\). Let \(z(x) = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}(x;\, s)\). Differentiating the ODE with respect to \(s\): \[z'' = f_y(x, y, y')\, z + f_{y'}(x, y, y')\, z'\] with initial conditions: \[z(a) = 0, \quad z'(a) = 1\] This is the variational equation (also called the sensitivity equation). Then: \[g'(s) = z(b;\, s)\] 14.3 Full Workflow Summary Set up the shooting problem and define \(g(s) = y(b; s) - d\) Apply Newton’s method to solve \(g(s^*) = 0\). For each Newton step: Solve the IVP to compute \(y(x; s_n)\) and evaluate \(g(s_n) = y(b; s_n) - d\) Solve the variational IVP to compute \(z(x; s_n)\) and evaluate \(g'(s_n) = z(b; s_n)\) Update: \(s_{n+1} = s_n - g(s_n)/g'(s_n)\) Reconstruct the BVP solution by solving the IVP with \(s = s^*\) 14.4 Required Tools An IVP solver (e.g., Runge–Kutta 4) A nonlinear equation solver for \(g(s) = 0\) (e.g., Newton’s method) 14.5 Example Problem: \(y'' = 2y^3 - 6y - 2x^3\), \(y(1) = 2\), \(y(2) = 5/2\) Exact solution: \(y(x) = x + \frac{1}{x}\), \(x \in [1, 2]\) Shooting setup: Solve the IVP: \(y'' = 2y^3 - 6y - 2x^3\), \(y(1) = 2\), \(y'(1) = s\) Define \(g(s) = y(2; s) - 5/2\). We want \(g(s^*) = 0\). Variational equation: \[z'' = (6y^2 - 6)\, z, \quad z(1) = 0, \quad z'(1) = 1\] Newton update: \[s_{n+1} = s_n - \frac{g(s_n)}{g'(s_n)} = s_n - \frac{y(2; s_n) - 5/2}{z(2; s_n)}\]
review-lu-decomposition-for-finite-difference-method.html
15 Review: LU Decomposition (for Finite Difference Method) 15.1 LU Decomposition 15.2 Tridiagonal Matrices

15 Review: LU Decomposition (for Finite Difference Method) The last step of finite difference methods (and Newton iterations) requires solving a linear system \(Ax = b\). 15.1 LU Decomposition Decompose \(A = LU\) where \(L\) is lower triangular and \(U\) is upper triangular: Factor: Compute \(L\), \(U\) such that \(A = LU\) — costs \(\mathcal{O}(N^3)\) for dense \(N \times N\) matrices Forward substitution: Solve \(Ly = b\) — costs \(\mathcal{O}(N^2)\) Back substitution: Solve \(Ux = y\) — costs \(\mathcal{O}(N^2)\) 15.2 Tridiagonal Matrices For a tridiagonal matrix \(A\) (bandwidth \(m = 3\)): \[A = \begin{pmatrix} \beta_1 & \gamma_1 & & \\ \alpha_2 & \beta_2 & \gamma_2 & \\ & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots \\ & & \alpha_N & \beta_N \end{pmatrix}\] LU factorization costs \(\mathcal{O}(N)\) operations (vs \(\mathcal{O}(N^3)\) for dense) Back/forward substitution also costs \(\mathcal{O}(N)\) Bandwidth \(m = m_1 + m_2 + 1\), where \(m_1\) = lower bands, \(m_2\) = upper bands. LU of a banded matrix costs \(\mathcal{O}(m^2 N)\) — much cheaper for small \(m\).
method-2-finite-difference-method-for-bvps.html
16 Method 2: Finite Difference Method for BVPs 16.1 Overview 16.2 Model Problem 16.3 Discretization 16.4 Linear System 16.5 Neumann Boundary Conditions

16 Method 2: Finite Difference Method for BVPs 16.1 Overview Instead of converting to an IVP, we discretize the domain and compute all solution values simultaneously by solving a linear system. 16.2 Model Problem Consider the model BVP with zero Dirichlet boundary conditions: \[-u'' = f(x), \quad x \in [0, 1], \quad u(0) = 0, \quad u(1) = 0\] The exact solution is: \[u(x) = C_1 + C_2 x - \int_0^x F(s)\, ds, \quad F(s) = \int_0^s f(t)\, dt\] where \(C_1\), \(C_2\) are determined by the boundary conditions. 16.3 Discretization Uniform grid: \(x_j = jh\), \(h = 1/N\), \(j = 0, 1, \ldots, N\) Second-order central difference approximation to \(u''(x_j)\): \[u''(x_j) \approx \frac{u_{j-1} - 2u_j + u_{j+1}}{h^2}\] The discrete equations are: \[\frac{-u_{j-1} + 2u_j - u_{j+1}}{h^2} = f(x_j), \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\] with \(u_0 = u_N = 0\) from boundary conditions. 16.4 Linear System Let \(\mathbf{u} = (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_{N-1})^T\) and \(\mathbf{f} = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_{N-1})^T\). The system is: \[A_{fd}\, \mathbf{u} = h^2 \mathbf{f}\] where \(A_{fd}\) is the tridiagonal matrix: \[A_{fd} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & & \\ -1 & 2 & -1 & \\ & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots \\ & & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\] This matrix is symmetric positive definite, so \(A_{fd}\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{f}\) has a unique solution. 16.5 Neumann Boundary Conditions For BCs of the form \(u'(0) = c\), \(u'(1) = d\): Naive approach (one-sided difference, first-order): \[\frac{u_1 - u_0}{h} = c, \quad \frac{u_N - u_{N-1}}{h} = d\] Preferred approach — introduce ghost points \(x_{-1}\) and \(x_{N+1}\): Use the centered difference \(\frac{u_1 - u_{-1}}{2h} = c\), giving \(u_{-1} = u_1 - 2hc\). This maintains second-order accuracy and can be eliminated from the system.
method-3-midpoint-discretization.html
17 Method 3: Midpoint Discretization 17.1 Overview 17.2 Grid Setup 17.3 Midpoint Scheme 17.4 Advantages 17.5 Nonlinear Case

17 Method 3: Midpoint Discretization 17.1 Overview For the first-order system: \[Y' = F(x, Y), \quad B[Y(x_0), Y(x_N)] = \tau\] the midpoint scheme discretizes at cell midpoints rather than grid points, which simplifies handling of non-uniform grids. 17.2 Grid Setup Define grid points \(x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_N\) with midpoints: \[x_{j+1/2} = \frac{x_j + x_{j+1}}{2}, \quad h_j = x_{j+1} - x_j\] 17.3 Midpoint Scheme \[\frac{Y_{j+1} - Y_j}{h_j} = F\!\left(x_{j+1/2},\, \frac{Y_j + Y_{j+1}}{2}\right), \quad j = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1\] This gives \(N \cdot m\) equations from the ODE (where \(m\) is the system dimension). Combined with \(m\) boundary condition equations, we have: \[(N+1) \cdot m \text{ equations for } (N+1) \cdot m \text{ unknowns}\] This is a complete system. 17.4 Advantages Only involves first-order derivatives — simpler discretization Handles non-uniform grids naturally (two-point stencil: \(x_j\) and \(x_{j+1}\)) Works well for general first-order BVP systems 17.5 Nonlinear Case If \(F\) is nonlinear in \(Y\), apply Newton’s method to the resulting nonlinear system.
consistency-convergence-and-stability-of-fdm-for-bvps.html
18 Consistency, Convergence, and Stability of FDM for BVPs 18.1 Consistency 18.2 Stability for BVPs 18.3 Convergence

18 Consistency, Convergence, and Stability of FDM for BVPs 18.1 Consistency Local Truncation Error (LTE): For the model problem \(u'' = f\), \(u(0) = u(1) = 0\): \[\tau_j = \frac{Y(x_{j+1}) - 2Y(x_j) + Y(x_{j-1})}{h^2} - f(x_j)\] By Taylor expansion: \[\tau_j = \frac{h^2}{12} Y^{(4)}(x_j) + \mathcal{O}(h^4)\] The method is consistent with order 2 (LTE \(= \mathcal{O}(h^2)\)). 18.2 Stability for BVPs Definition: A finite difference method for a linear BVP producing matrix equations \(A_{fd}^{(h)} \mathbf{u}_h = \mathbf{f}_h\) is stable if: \((A_{fd}^{(h)})^{-1}\) exists for all sufficiently small \(h\) There exists a constant \(C\) (independent of \(h\)) such that \(\|(A_{fd}^{(h)})^{-1}\| \leq C\) for all \(h \leq h_0\) 18.3 Convergence Given the error equation: \[A_{fd}(\mathbf{u}_{exact} - \mathbf{u}_h) = h^2 \boldsymbol{\tau}\] where \(\boldsymbol{\tau}\) is the vector of local truncation errors, we have: \[\|\mathbf{u}_{exact} - \mathbf{u}_h\| = \|(A_{fd})^{-1}\| \cdot \|h^2 \boldsymbol{\tau}\|\] If the method is stable (\(\|(A_{fd})^{-1}\| \leq C\)) and consistent (LTE \(= \mathcal{O}(h^2)\)), then: \[\|\mathbf{u}_{exact} - \mathbf{u}_h\| = \mathcal{O}(h^2)\] For BVPs: Stability + Consistency \(\Rightarrow\) Convergence (analogous to the Lax Equivalence Theorem for IVPs).
method-4-galerkin-weak-formulation.html
19 Method 4: Galerkin / Weak Formulation 19.1 Motivation 19.2 Weak Formulation 19.3 Remark: Navier–Stokes Equations 19.4 Galerkin Method

19 Method 4: Galerkin / Weak Formulation 19.1 Motivation Consider the BVP: \[-y'' + cy = f(x), \quad x \in [0,1], \quad y(0) = y(1) = 0\] A classical (strong) solution requires \(y \in C^2[0,1]\). However: Strong solutions may not exist for discontinuous \(f\) or when shocks are present The regularity requirement is analytically inconvenient 19.2 Weak Formulation Derivation: Multiply the ODE by a smooth test function \(\phi \in C_0^\infty(0,1)\) and integrate by parts: \[\int_0^1 y'' \phi\, dx = [y'\phi]_0^1 - \int_0^1 y'\phi'\, dx = -\int_0^1 y'\phi'\, dx \quad (\phi = 0 \text{ at boundary})\] This gives the weak form: find \(y \in H_0^1(0,1)\) such that \[a(y, \phi) = \langle f, \phi \rangle \quad \forall \phi \in H_0^1(0,1)\] where: - \(a(u, v) = \int_0^1 u'v'\, dx + c\int_0^1 uv\, dx\) (bilinear form) \(\langle f, v \rangle = \int_0^1 f(x)v(x)\, dx\) (linear form / \(L^2\) inner product) \(H_0^1(0,1) = \{v \in L^2 : v' \in L^2,\; v(0) = v(1) = 0\}\) (Sobolev space) Key insight: The weak formulation only requires one derivative of \(y\) (not two), so it admits a broader class of solutions. 19.3 Remark: Navier–Stokes Equations The Navier–Stokes equations (governing viscous fluid motion, used in car/aircraft design and ocean current modeling) have proven weak solutions, but the regularity (smoothness) of these solutions is one of the 7 Millennium Prize Problems in mathematics. 19.4 Galerkin Method 19.4.1 Approximation Space Introduce a finite-dimensional subspace \(V_h \subset H_0^1(0,1)\): \[V_h = \text{span}\{\phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_N\}\] where \(\{\phi_i\}\) are linearly independent basis functions. 19.4.2 Discrete Problem Find \(u_h = \sum_{j=1}^N \mu_j \phi_j \in V_h\) such that: \[a(u_h, \phi_i) = \langle f, \phi_i \rangle, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N\] This gives the linear system: \[A\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{F}\] where: \(A_{ij} = a(\phi_j, \phi_i)\) — the stiffness matrix \(F_i = \langle f, \phi_i \rangle\) — the load vector 19.4.3 Céa’s Lemma (Error Estimate) Assume \(V\) is a Hilbert space and \(a(\cdot, \cdot)\) satisfies: Coercivity: \(a(v,v) \geq \alpha_0 \|v\|_V^2\) for some \(\alpha_0 > 0\) Continuity: \(|a(u,v)| \leq M\|u\|_V\|v\|_V\) Boundedness: \(|\langle f, v\rangle| \leq K\|f\|\|v\|_V\) Then: \[\|u - u_h\|_V \leq \frac{M}{\alpha_0} \min_{w_h \in V_h} \|u - w_h\|_V\] The Galerkin error is quasi-optimal — it is bounded by a constant times the best approximation error in \(V_h\).
finite-element-method-fem.html
20 Finite Element Method (FEM) 20.1 Basis Function Choice 20.2 Spectral Galerkin Method 20.3 Comparison

20 Finite Element Method (FEM) 20.1 Basis Function Choice The key choice in the Galerkin method is the basis \(\{\phi_i\}\) for \(V_h\). 20.1.1 Piecewise Linear “Hat” Functions The simplest FEM basis: hat functions \(\phi_i\) defined on a grid \(x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_N\): \[\phi_i(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{x - x_{i-1}}{x_i - x_{i-1}} & x \in [x_{i-1}, x_i] \\ \frac{x_{i+1} - x}{x_{i+1} - x_i} & x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}] \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\] Properties: \(\phi_i(x_j) = \delta_{ij}\) (equals 1 at node \(x_i\), 0 at all others) Local support: \(\phi_i\) is nonzero only on \([x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}]\) 20.1.2 Sparsity of the Stiffness Matrix Because hat functions have local support: \[A_{ij} = a(\phi_j, \phi_i) \neq 0 \quad \text{only if } |i - j| \leq 1\] The stiffness matrix is tridiagonal — the linear system can be solved in \(\mathcal{O}(N)\) operations. 20.2 Spectral Galerkin Method Use global Fourier basis functions: \[\phi_k(x) = e^{2\pi i k x}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N\] Properties: Global support — each basis function is nonzero everywhere Leads to a dense stiffness matrix, but exponential convergence for smooth solutions Better suited for problems with smooth solutions on simple domains 20.3 Comparison Method Basis Stiffness Matrix Convergence FEM (hat functions) Piecewise polynomial, local support Sparse (tridiagonal) Algebraic in \(h\) Spectral Galerkin Global Fourier modes Dense Exponential (smooth solutions)
summary.html
21 Summary

21 Summary Method Approach When to Use Shooting Method Convert BVP \(\to\) IVP; use Newton’s method to find \(s^*\) Simple BVPs; need IVP solver + rootfinder Finite Difference (FDM) Discretize ODE at grid points; solve tridiagonal system Standard BVPs with uniform grids Midpoint Discretization Discretize first-order system at midpoints Non-uniform grids; general first-order systems Galerkin / FEM Variational formulation; solve in finite-dimensional subspace Problems lacking strong solutions; complex geometry Key Takeaways: BVPs can have zero, one, or multiple solutions — no general existence/uniqueness theory Stability for BVPs \(\ne\) stability for IVPs The shooting method relies on Newton’s method and the variational (sensitivity) equation FDM for BVPs yields sparse (tridiagonal) linear systems, solved efficiently via LU factorization The Galerkin weak formulation extends solvability beyond classical \(C^2\) solutions Céa’s lemma guarantees quasi-optimal FEM approximations
